Here's a one-page PRD outline for a dark-mode theme.

# PRD: Dark Mode Theme

**Author:** [you] · **Status:** Draft · **Last updated:** 2026-06-22

## 1. Summary
Add a user-selectable dark color theme to the web app, with a light/dark/system toggle that persists across sessions.

## 2. Problem & Motivation
- Users in low-light environments report eye strain on the current light-only UI.
- Dark mode is a baseline expectation for modern web apps; its absence shows up in user feedback and reviews.
- OS-level dark mode is widespread, and the app currently ignores the user's system preference.

## 3. Goals
- Ship a dark theme that covers all primary screens and components.
- Offer three modes: Light, Dark, and System (follows OS setting).
- Persist the choice per user/device and apply it before first paint (no flash of wrong theme).

## 4. Non-Goals
- Custom/user-defined color themes or per-component theming.
- Theming of emails, PDFs, or other exported artifacts.
- A full design-system overhaul beyond what dark mode requires.

## 5. Users & Use Cases
- Night/low-light users who want reduced brightness.
- Users whose OS is set to dark and expect the app to match automatically.
- Accessibility-sensitive users needing adequate contrast.

## 6. Requirements
**Functional**
- Toggle control in settings (and/or top nav) with Light / Dark / System options.
- "System" tracks OS preference live (`prefers-color-scheme`) without a reload.
- Preference persists (stored per account if logged in, else local storage).
- Theme applies on initial load with no flash of incorrect theme.

**Design / Visual**
- Define semantic color tokens (background, surface, text, border, accent, states) for both themes.
- All text/icon pairs meet WCAG AA contrast in both themes.
- Cover images, charts, shadows, and illustrations that don't auto-adapt.

**Technical**
- Theme driven by tokens/CSS variables, not hardcoded colors.
- No measurable impact on load performance.

## 7. Success Metrics
- % of active users who enable dark or system mode within 30 days.
- Reduction in eye-strain / theme-related support and review mentions.
- No regression in contrast/accessibility audits or Core Web Vitals.

## 8. Rollout Plan
- Phase 1: Token system + core screens behind a feature flag (internal).
- Phase 2: Full component coverage + QA across browsers and devices.
- Phase 3: Gradual rollout, then default "System" for new users.

## 9. Open Questions
- Default for existing users: keep Light or switch to System?
- Account-level sync across devices, or device-local only?
- Which screens or third-party embeds are out of scope for v1?

## 10. Dependencies & Risks
- Risk: inconsistent colors from hardcoded values → mitigated by tokenizing first.
- Dependency: design sign-off on the dark palette and contrast.
- Risk: third-party/embedded widgets that don't support theming.

Want me to drop this into a Markdown file, or tailor any section (metrics, palette, rollout) to your actual app?

# PRD: Dark Mode Theme

**Author:** [you] · **Status:** Draft · **Last updated:** 2026-06-22

## 1. Summary
Add a user-selectable dark color theme to the web app, with a light/dark/system toggle that persists across sessions.

**Recommendation:** Build dark mode now as a token-driven theme with a Light/Dark/System toggle, defaulting to **System** for new users and keeping existing users on Light until they opt in. Ship behind a feature flag, store the preference per account (with local-storage fallback), and treat WCAG AA contrast as a launch gate. This is the call we're asking reviewers to accept or veto.

## 2. Problem & Motivation
- Users in low-light environments report eye strain on the current light-only UI.
- Dark mode is a baseline expectation for modern web apps; its absence shows up in user feedback and reviews.
- OS-level dark mode is widespread, and the app currently ignores the user's system preference.

## 3. Goals
- Ship a dark theme that covers all primary screens and components.
- Offer three modes: Light, Dark, and System (follows OS setting).
- Persist the choice per user/device and apply it before first paint (no flash of wrong theme).

## 4. Non-Goals
- Custom/user-defined color themes or per-component theming.
- Theming of emails, PDFs, or other exported artifacts.
- A full design-system overhaul beyond what dark mode requires.

## 5. Users & Use Cases
- Night/low-light users who want reduced brightness.
- Users whose OS is set to dark and expect the app to match automatically.
- Accessibility-sensitive users needing adequate contrast.

## 6. Requirements
**Functional**
- Toggle control in settings (and/or top nav) with Light / Dark / System options.
- "System" tracks OS preference live (`prefers-color-scheme`) without a reload.
- Preference persists (stored per account if logged in, else local storage).
- Theme applies on initial load with no flash of incorrect theme.

**Design / Visual**
- Define semantic color tokens (background, surface, text, border, accent, states) for both themes.
- All text/icon pairs meet WCAG AA contrast in both themes.
- Cover images, charts, shadows, and illustrations that don't auto-adapt.

**Technical**
- Theme driven by tokens/CSS variables, not hardcoded colors.
- No measurable impact on load performance.

## 7. Success Metrics
- % of active users who enable dark or system mode within 30 days.
- Reduction in eye-strain / theme-related support and review mentions.
- No regression in contrast/accessibility audits or Core Web Vitals.

## 8. Rollout Plan
- Phase 1: Token system + core screens behind a feature flag (internal).
- Phase 2: Full component coverage + QA across browsers and devices.
- Phase 3: Gradual rollout, then default "System" for new users.

## 9. Open Questions
- Default for existing users: keep Light or switch to System? **Recommend keeping existing users on Light** to avoid a surprise visual change, prompting them once to try System.
- Account-level sync across devices, or device-local only? **Recommend account-level sync** for logged-in users, with local storage as the fallback.
- Which screens or third-party embeds are out of scope for v1? **Recommend excluding third-party embeds and exported artifacts** from v1.

## 10. Dependencies & Risks
- Risk: inconsistent colors from hardcoded values → mitigated by tokenizing first.
- Dependency: design sign-off on the dark palette and contrast.
- Risk: third-party/embedded widgets that don't support theming.

# PRD: Dark Mode Theme

**Author:** _[owner]_ · **Status:** Draft · **Last updated:** 2026-06-22

## 1. Summary
Add a user-selectable dark theme with a Light / Dark / System toggle that persists across sessions and applies before first paint. Scope is a faithful dark rendering of existing screens — not a redesign.

## 2. Problem & Motivation
- Low-light users report eye strain on the current light-only UI.
- Dark mode is a baseline expectation for modern web apps; its absence recurs in feedback and store reviews.
- Many users run their OS in dark mode, and the app currently ignores that preference.

## 3. Goals
- Ship a dark theme covering all primary screens and shared components.
- Offer three modes: Light, Dark, and System (follows the OS setting live).
- Persist the choice and apply it before first paint — no flash of the wrong theme.

## 4. Non-Goals
- Custom or user-defined palettes; per-component theming.
- Theming of emails, PDFs, or other exported artifacts.
- A design-system overhaul beyond what dark mode requires.

## 5. Users & Use Cases
- Night / low-light users who want reduced brightness.
- Users whose OS is dark and expect the app to match automatically.
- Accessibility-sensitive users who need adequate contrast in either theme.

## 6. Requirements

**Functional**
- Toggle in Settings (and optionally top nav) offering Light / Dark / System.
- "System" tracks `prefers-color-scheme` live, with no reload.
- Preference persists per account when logged in, else in local storage.
- Theme is set before first paint (e.g. an inline head script that applies the theme class from the stored value) so there is no flash.

**Design / Visual**
- Define semantic color tokens — background, surface, text, border, accent, and state colors — for both themes.
- All text and icon pairs meet WCAG AA contrast in both themes.
- Handle assets that don't auto-adapt: images, charts, shadows, illustrations.

**Technical**
- Colors come from tokens / CSS variables, never hardcoded values.
- No measurable regression in load performance or Core Web Vitals.

## 7. Success Metrics
- **Adoption:** % of active users on Dark or System within 30 days of launch (target TBD against baseline).
- **Quality:** drop in eye-strain / theme-related support tickets and review mentions.
- **Guardrail:** no regression in accessibility (contrast) audits or Core Web Vitals.

## 8. Rollout Plan
- **Phase 1:** Token system + core screens behind a feature flag (internal only).
- **Phase 2:** Full component coverage + QA across browsers and devices.
- **Phase 3:** Gradual rollout; default new users to System.

## 9. Open Questions
- Default for existing users — keep Light, or move them to System?
- Account-level sync across devices, or device-local only?
- Which screens or third-party embeds are out of scope for v1?

## 10. Dependencies & Risks
- **Risk:** hardcoded colors produce inconsistent dark output → mitigated by tokenizing first.
- **Risk:** third-party / embedded widgets that don't support theming → identify and isolate in Phase 2.
- **Dependency:** design sign-off on the dark palette and contrast.
